title: Building an Internal Developer Platform from Scratch
description: A step-by-step checklist for designing and building an internal developer platform (IDP) that gives your engineers self-service access to infrastructure, environments, and deployments without filing tickets.

items.title: Map your developers' biggest time sinks
items.description: Before building anything, figure out where developers waste time. Common culprits: waiting for environments, writing boilerplate configs, asking ops to provision resources. Run a quick survey or check your ticket system for the most frequent requests. Build the platform around real pain, not theoretical improvements.

codeBlocks.label: Find the most common ops/infra tickets (Jira example)
codeBlocks.code:
# Pull the top request types from the last 90 days
curl -s -u "$JIRA_USER:$JIRA_TOKEN" \
  "https://your-org.atlassian.net/rest/api/3/search?jql=project=OPS+AND+created>=-90d&maxResults=100" \
  | jq '[.issues[].fields.summary] | group_by(.) | map({request: .[0], count: length}) | sort_by(-.count) | .[:10]'

# Example output:
# [
#   {"request": "Create new dev environment", "count": 34},
#   {"request": "Add database for service X", "count": 28},
#   {"request": "Update nginx config", "count": 22},
#   {"request": "Grant access to staging", "count": 19}
# ]

codeBlocks.label: Quick developer survey template
codeBlocks.code:
# Questions to ask your team:
# 1. What takes the longest when starting a new service?
# 2. What do you need ops help for that you wish was self-service?
# 3. How long does it take to get a working dev environment?
# 4. What manual steps do you repeat for every deployment?
# 5. Where do you go to find docs about your service dependencies?

items.title: Define your platform's API contract
items.description: Your IDP needs a clear interface that developers interact with. This is usually a combination of a service catalog, templates, and APIs. Define what actions developers can take (create a service, spin up a database, deploy to staging) before picking any tools. The API contract is your platform's product - everything else is implementation detail.

codeBlocks.label: Platform API contract example
codeBlocks.code:
# platform-api-contract.yaml
# Define what your IDP exposes to developers
platform:
  actions:
    - name: create-service
      inputs:
        - service_name: string
        - language: [go, python, node, java]
        - team: string
      outputs:
        - git_repo_url
        - ci_pipeline_url
        - dev_environment_url

    - name: provision-database
      inputs:
        - engine: [postgres, mysql, redis]
        - size: [small, medium, large]
        - environment: [dev, staging, prod]
      outputs:
        - connection_string
        - credentials_secret_path

    - name: deploy
      inputs:
        - service_name: string
        - environment: [dev, staging, prod]
        - version: string
      outputs:
        - deployment_url
        - rollback_command

items.title: Set up a service catalog with Backstage
items.description: A service catalog is the front door of your IDP. Developers need one place to see all services, who owns them, their status, and their docs. Backstage by Spotify is the most adopted open-source option. It is not the only choice, but it has the largest ecosystem of plugins and community support.

codeBlocks.label: Bootstrap a Backstage instance
codeBlocks.code:
# Install Backstage CLI and create a new app
npx @backstage/create-app@latest

# Follow the prompts:
# ? Enter a name for the app: my-platform

cd my-platform

# Start the dev server
yarn dev

# Backstage runs on http://localhost:3000 (frontend)
# and http://localhost:7007 (backend)

codeBlocks.label: Register a service in the catalog
codeBlocks.code:
# catalog-info.yaml (lives in each service's repo root)
apiVersion: backstage.io/v1alpha1
kind: Component
metadata:
  name: payment-service
  description: Handles payment processing via Stripe
  annotations:
    github.com/project-slug: my-org/payment-service
    backstage.io/techdocs-ref: dir:.
  tags:
    - python
    - payments
  links:
    - url: https://grafana.internal/d/payment-service
      title: Grafana Dashboard
    - url: https://pagerduty.com/services/P123ABC
      title: PagerDuty
spec:
  type: service
  lifecycle: production
  owner: team-payments
  system: checkout
  dependsOn:
    - resource:payments-db
    - component:user-service
  providesApis:
    - payments-api

items.title: Build golden path templates for new services
items.description: Every new service at your company should start from a template that includes CI/CD, logging, health checks, and standard configs out of the box. This is the highest-impact feature of an IDP. Developers pick a template, fill in a few fields, and get a production-ready repo in minutes instead of days of copy-paste from other services.

codeBlocks.label: Backstage scaffolder template definition
codeBlocks.code:
# templates/go-service/template.yaml
apiVersion: scaffolder.backstage.io/v1beta3
kind: Template
metadata:
  name: go-service
  title: Go Microservice
  description: Creates a Go service with CI/CD, Dockerfile, and Kubernetes manifests
  tags:
    - go
    - recommended
spec:
  owner: platform-team
  type: service
  parameters:
    - title: Service Info
      required:
        - name
        - owner
      properties:
        name:
          title: Service Name
          type: string
          pattern: '^[a-z0-9-]+$'
        owner:
          title: Owner Team
          type: string
          ui:field: OwnerPicker
        description:
          title: Description
          type: string
  steps:
    - id: fetch-template
      name: Fetch Template
      action: fetch:template
      input:
        url: ./skeleton
        values:
          name: ${{ parameters.name }}
          owner: ${{ parameters.owner }}
    - id: publish
      name: Create GitHub Repo
      action: publish:github
      input:
        repoUrl: github.com?owner=my-org&repo=${{ parameters.name }}
        defaultBranch: main
    - id: register
      name: Register in Catalog
      action: catalog:register
      input:
        repoContentsUrl: ${{ steps.publish.output.repoContentsUrl }}
        catalogInfoPath: /catalog-info.yaml

codeBlocks.label: Template skeleton directory structure
codeBlocks.code:
# templates/go-service/skeleton/
.
├── catalog-info.yaml
├── Dockerfile
├── .github/
│   └── workflows/
│       └── ci.yaml
├── cmd/
│   └── server/
│       └── main.go
├── internal/
│   └── handler/
│       └── health.go
├── k8s/
│   ├── deployment.yaml
│   ├── service.yaml
│   └── ingress.yaml
├── go.mod
├── go.sum
├── Makefile
└── README.md

items.title: Wire up self-service infrastructure provisioning
items.description: Developers should not file tickets to get a database or a message queue. Use Crossplane or Terraform with a GitOps workflow to let them provision infrastructure through the platform. The platform team defines what is available and sets guardrails. Developers request resources through templates or a UI, and the platform handles the rest.

codeBlocks.label: Crossplane composition for a PostgreSQL database
codeBlocks.code:
# crossplane/compositions/postgres.yaml
apiVersion: apiextensions.crossplane.io/v1
kind: Composition
metadata:
  name: postgres-instance
spec:
  compositeTypeRef:
    apiVersion: database.platform.io/v1alpha1
    kind: PostgresInstance
  resources:
    - name: rds-instance
      base:
        apiVersion: rds.aws.upbound.io/v1beta1
        kind: Instance
        spec:
          forProvider:
            engine: postgres
            engineVersion: "16"
            instanceClass: db.t3.medium
            allocatedStorage: 20
            masterUsername: admin
            autoMinorVersionUpgrade: true
            backupRetentionPeriod: 7
            region: us-east-1
      patches:
        - fromFieldPath: spec.size
          toFieldPath: spec.forProvider.instanceClass
          transforms:
            - type: map
              map:
                small: db.t3.micro
                medium: db.t3.medium
                large: db.r6g.large
---
# What developers actually write:
apiVersion: database.platform.io/v1alpha1
kind: PostgresInstance
metadata:
  name: orders-db
spec:
  size: medium
  environment: staging

codeBlocks.label: Backstage plugin to trigger provisioning
codeBlocks.code:
# Add a provisioning step in your Backstage template
steps:
  - id: provision-db
    name: Provision PostgreSQL
    action: kubernetes:apply
    input:
      manifest:
        apiVersion: database.platform.io/v1alpha1
        kind: PostgresInstance
        metadata:
          name: ${{ parameters.name }}-db
          namespace: ${{ parameters.team }}
        spec:
          size: ${{ parameters.dbSize }}
          environment: dev

items.title: Automate ephemeral environment creation
items.description: Shared staging environments are a bottleneck. When three teams deploy to the same staging, they step on each other. Give developers on-demand preview environments that spin up per pull request and tear down when the PR closes. This removes the "can I deploy to staging?" Slack messages and speeds up the review cycle.

codeBlocks.label: GitHub Actions workflow for preview environments
codeBlocks.code:
# .github/workflows/preview-env.yaml
name: Preview Environment
on:
  pull_request:
    types: [opened, synchronize, reopened, closed]

jobs:
  deploy-preview:
    if: github.event.action != 'closed'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Build and push image
        run: |
          IMAGE="ghcr.io/${{ github.repository }}:pr-${{ github.event.number }}"
          docker build -t $IMAGE .
          docker push $IMAGE

      - name: Deploy preview environment
        run: |
          NAMESPACE="preview-pr-${{ github.event.number }}"
          kubectl create namespace $NAMESPACE --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -

          helm upgrade --install preview-${{ github.event.number }} ./chart \
            --namespace $NAMESPACE \
            --set image.tag=pr-${{ github.event.number }} \
            --set ingress.host=pr-${{ github.event.number }}.preview.example.com

      - name: Comment PR with environment URL
        uses: actions/github-script@v7
        with:
          script: |
            github.rest.issues.createComment({
              owner: context.repo.owner,
              repo: context.repo.repo,
              issue_number: context.issue.number,
              body: `Preview: https://pr-${context.issue.number}.preview.example.com`
            })

  teardown-preview:
    if: github.event.action == 'closed'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Delete preview environment
        run: |
          kubectl delete namespace preview-pr-${{ github.event.number }} --ignore-not-found

items.title: Build a portal UI that surfaces everything in one place
items.description: Developers should not need to context-switch between GitHub, Grafana, PagerDuty, ArgoCD, and Jira just to understand the state of their service. The portal pulls data from all these tools into a single view per service: recent deployments, open incidents, SLO status, and team ownership. Backstage plugins handle most of this.

codeBlocks.label: Install common Backstage plugins

items.title: Set up RBAC and team ownership boundaries
items.description: Without access controls, your platform is either too open (anyone can delete production databases) or too locked down (back to filing tickets). Map your org's team structure into the platform and set clear ownership. Every resource should have an owner. Every action should check permissions. Start strict and loosen based on feedback.

codeBlocks.label: Define teams and ownership in Backstage
codeBlocks.code:
# org-data/teams.yaml
apiVersion: backstage.io/v1alpha1
kind: Group
metadata:
  name: team-payments
  description: Handles all payment processing
spec:
  type: team
  profile:
    displayName: Payments Team
    slack: '#team-payments'
  children: []
  members:
    - user:jane.doe
    - user:john.smith
---
apiVersion: backstage.io/v1alpha1
kind: Group
metadata:
  name: platform-team
spec:
  type: team
  profile:
    displayName: Platform Team
  children: []
  members:
    - user:alice.engineer

codeBlocks.label: Kubernetes RBAC tied to team namespaces
codeBlocks.code:
# Each team gets their own namespace with scoped permissions
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: team-payments
  labels:
    team: payments
    managed-by: platform
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: team-payments-dev
  namespace: team-payments
subjects:
  - kind: Group
    name: team-payments
    apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
roleRef:
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: namespace-admin
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

items.title: Create shared CI/CD pipeline templates
items.description: If every team writes their own CI/CD pipeline from scratch, you end up with 50 slightly different ways to build and deploy. Create shared pipeline templates that teams inherit. They get security scanning, artifact publishing, and deployment steps for free. Teams can extend the templates but cannot skip the required steps.

codeBlocks.label: Reusable GitHub Actions workflow
codeBlocks.code:
# .github/workflows/platform-build.yaml (in a shared repo)
name: Platform Build
on:
  workflow_call:
    inputs:
      language:
        required: true
        type: string
      deploy_target:
        required: false
        type: string
        default: 'kubernetes'

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Run security scan
        uses: aquasecurity/trivy-action@master
        with:
          scan-type: fs
          exit-code: 1
          severity: HIGH,CRITICAL

      - name: Build container image
        run: |
          docker build -t ${{ github.repository }}:${{ github.sha }} .

      - name: Push to registry
        run: |
          echo ${{ secrets.REGISTRY_TOKEN }} | docker login ghcr.io -u ${{ github.actor }} --password-stdin
          docker push ghcr.io/${{ github.repository }}:${{ github.sha }}

      - name: Deploy to Kubernetes
        if: inputs.deploy_target == 'kubernetes'
        run: |
          kubectl set image deployment/${{ github.event.repository.name }} \
            app=ghcr.io/${{ github.repository }}:${{ github.sha }}

codeBlocks.label: How teams consume the shared workflow
codeBlocks.code:
# In each service repo: .github/workflows/ci.yaml
name: CI
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:

jobs:
  build-and-deploy:
    uses: my-org/platform-pipelines/.github/workflows/platform-build.yaml@main
    with:
      language: go
      deploy_target: kubernetes
    secrets: inherit

items.title: Integrate secrets management into the platform
items.description: Developers should never copy secrets from Slack messages or shared spreadsheets. Wire a secrets manager into your platform so developers can request and access secrets through a standard interface. The platform injects secrets at runtime - developers never see the raw values in configs or environment variables.

codeBlocks.label: Set up External Secrets Operator with AWS Secrets Manager
codeBlocks.code:
# Install External Secrets Operator
helm repo add external-secrets https://charts.external-secrets.io
helm install external-secrets external-secrets/external-secrets \
  -n external-secrets --create-namespace

# Create a SecretStore pointing to AWS
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: external-secrets.io/v1beta1
kind: ClusterSecretStore
metadata:
  name: aws-secrets
spec:
  provider:
    aws:
      service: SecretsManager
      region: us-east-1
      auth:
        jwt:
          serviceAccountRef:
            name: external-secrets-sa
            namespace: external-secrets
EOF

codeBlocks.label: How developers request a secret
codeBlocks.code:
# Developers add this to their service's k8s manifests:
apiVersion: external-secrets.io/v1beta1
kind: ExternalSecret
metadata:
  name: payment-service-secrets
  namespace: team-payments
spec:
  refreshInterval: 1h
  secretStoreRef:
    name: aws-secrets
    kind: ClusterSecretStore
  target:
    name: payment-service-secrets
  data:
    - secretKey: STRIPE_API_KEY
      remoteRef:
        key: prod/payments/stripe
        property: api_key
    - secretKey: DATABASE_URL
      remoteRef:
        key: prod/payments/database
        property: connection_string

items.title: Set SLOs for the platform itself
items.description: Your platform is a product. It needs SLOs just like any production service. If service creation takes 30 minutes, that is your problem. Track things like: time to create a new service, time to provision a database, portal uptime, and deployment pipeline duration. If you do not measure these, you will not know if the platform is helping or hurting.

codeBlocks.label: Platform SLO definitions
codeBlocks.code:
# platform-slos.yaml
slos:
  - name: service-creation-time
    description: Time from template selection to working service
    target: 95th percentile under 10 minutes
    measurement: backstage_scaffolder_task_duration_seconds

  - name: environment-provisioning
    description: Time to spin up a preview environment
    target: 95th percentile under 5 minutes
    measurement: preview_env_creation_duration_seconds

  - name: portal-availability
    description: Backstage portal uptime
    target: 99.9% monthly
    measurement: backstage_http_request_duration_seconds

  - name: pipeline-duration
    description: Shared CI/CD pipeline p95 duration
    target: 95th percentile under 15 minutes
    measurement: github_actions_workflow_duration_seconds

  - name: secret-sync-latency
    description: Time for a secret update to reach pods
    target: 99th percentile under 5 minutes
    measurement: external_secrets_sync_duration_seconds

codeBlocks.label: Track platform SLOs in Prometheus
codeBlocks.code:
# Grafana query for service creation time SLO
histogram_quantile(0.95,
  rate(backstage_scaffolder_task_duration_seconds_bucket[7d])
)

# Alert when SLO is at risk
# promql: burn rate alert
(
  sum(rate(backstage_http_request_duration_seconds_count{code=~"5.."}[1h]))
  /
  sum(rate(backstage_http_request_duration_seconds_count[1h]))
) > 0.001

items.title: Generate docs from code and serve them in the portal
items.description: Documentation that lives in Confluence gets stale. Documentation that lives next to the code stays current because it is part of the same pull request. Use TechDocs (Backstage's docs plugin) to render Markdown from each repo and serve it in the portal. Developers write docs in their repo and the platform publishes them automatically.

codeBlocks.label: Set up TechDocs in a service repo
codeBlocks.code:
# mkdocs.yml in the service repo root
site_name: Payment Service
nav:
  - Home: index.md
  - Architecture: architecture.md
  - API Reference: api.md
  - Runbooks:
      - On-Call Guide: runbooks/oncall.md
      - Incident Response: runbooks/incidents.md

plugins:
  - techdocs-core

# Then in catalog-info.yaml, add:
# metadata:
#   annotations:
#     backstage.io/techdocs-ref: dir:.

codeBlocks.label: Build and preview docs locally
codeBlocks.code:
# Install the TechDocs CLI
npm install -g @techdocs/cli

# Generate and preview docs
techdocs-cli generate
techdocs-cli serve

# Output:
# Starting local server at http://localhost:3000
# Serving docs for payment-service

items.title: Add per-team cost visibility
items.description: Self-service infrastructure is great until the cloud bill triples. Tag every resource the platform creates with team and service labels, then surface costs per team in the portal. Developers who can see their costs make better decisions. Set budget alerts so teams know when they are trending above expectations.

codeBlocks.label: Enforce cost labels on all platform-managed resources
codeBlocks.code:
# Kyverno policy to require cost labels
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-cost-labels
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Enforce
  rules:
    - name: check-labels
      match:
        any:
          - resources:
              kinds:
                - Deployment
                - StatefulSet
                - Service
      validate:
        message: "All resources must have 'team' and 'service' labels for cost tracking"
        pattern:
          metadata:
            labels:
              team: "?*"
              service: "?*"

codeBlocks.label: Query AWS costs by team tag
codeBlocks.code:
# Get cost breakdown by team for last month
aws ce get-cost-and-usage \
  --time-period Start=2026-03-01,End=2026-03-31 \
  --granularity MONTHLY \
  --metrics BlendedCost \
  --group-by Type=TAG,Key=team \
  --query '{"And": [{"Tags": {"Key": "managed-by", "Values": ["platform"]}}]}' \
  | jq '.ResultsByTime[].Groups[] | {team: .Keys[0], cost: .Metrics.BlendedCost.Amount}'

# Example output:
# {"team": "team-payments", "cost": "1,247.83"}
# {"team": "team-search", "cost": "3,891.22"}
# {"team": "team-auth", "cost": "412.56"}

items.title: Track adoption and measure developer satisfaction
items.description: An IDP that nobody uses is wasted effort. Track how many teams are using the platform, which features get the most usage, and whether developers are actually faster. Run quarterly developer satisfaction surveys (keep them short - 5 questions max). The best signal is whether developers choose to use the platform when they have other options.

codeBlocks.label: Platform adoption dashboard metrics
codeBlocks.code:
# Key metrics to track in your Grafana dashboard
metrics:
  adoption:
    - name: services_in_catalog
      query: count(backstage_catalog_entities{kind="Component"})
      target: 80% of all services registered within 6 months

    - name: templates_used_weekly
      query: sum(increase(backstage_scaffolder_tasks_total[7d]))
      target: At least 3 new services per week via templates

    - name: self_service_ratio
      query: |
        platform_automated_requests / 
        (platform_automated_requests + manual_ops_tickets)
      target: Above 70% self-service within 3 months

  developer_experience:
    - name: time_to_first_deploy
      description: How long from repo creation to first production deploy
      target: Under 1 day

    - name: dora_deployment_frequency
      description: How often teams deploy per week
      target: Improving quarter over quarter

codeBlocks.label: Quick adoption check script
codeBlocks.code:
#!/bin/bash
# platform-health-check.sh
echo "=== Platform Adoption Report ==="
echo ""

# Count services in catalog
SERVICES=$(curl -s http://backstage.internal/api/catalog/entities?filter=kind=component | jq length)
echo "Services in catalog: $SERVICES"

# Templates used this month
TEMPLATES=$(curl -s http://backstage.internal/api/scaffolder/v2/tasks | jq '[.tasks[] | select(.createdAt > "2026-03-01")] | length')
echo "Templates used this month: $TEMPLATES"

# Average deploy time
echo ""
echo "Deploy times (p95, last 7 days):"
curl -s "http://prometheus:9090/api/v1/query?query=histogram_quantile(0.95,rate(deploy_duration_seconds_bucket[7d]))" \
  | jq '.data.result[] | {service: .metric.service, p95_seconds: .value[1]}'
